ip_outcome_note: Card name, type line (Saiyan/Earthling / Hirudegarn Saga), and characteristic lavender spiky hair in teal jacket confirm this is Trunks (youth). The card is explicitly titled 'Trunks // Iron Vow Trunks' — the character name is on the card itself.

trivia_bullets.text: BT4-023 is a dual-sided Leader card from Colossal Warfare (DBS-B04), the fourth main booster set for the Dragon Ball Super Card Game, released July 13, 2018 — at the time the largest DBS set with 218 total cards (124 unique) [Cardmarket: Colossal Warfare, The Games Corner release listing]. The set drew heavily on the Hirudegarn Saga from Dragon Ball Z: Wrath of the Dragon (1995 film), anchoring Trunks as the primary blue-energy Leader for that saga.

As a Leader card, BT4-023 flips to 'Iron Vow Trunks' (15,000 power) when life drops to 4 or less — the Awaken threshold. The reverse side's end-of-turn ability switches both an eligible blue Battle Card and one energy to Active Mode, making Iron Vow Trunks a strong blue-energy-acceleration engine in the Hirudegarn Saga leader slot [dbs-cardgame.com official card list, ToyWiz product listing].

The sword Tapion gives to the young Trunks at the end of Dragon Ball Z: Wrath of the Dragon is widely noted to resemble Future Trunks's iconic blade — Tapion's line that the sword 'always belonged to Trunks' is an in-universe nod to the connection between the two timelines, though the film sits outside the main manga canon [Wikipedia: Dragon Ball Z: Wrath of the Dragon, CBR: Where Did Trunks Get His Sword]. This context explains why BT4 pairs Trunks specifically with the Hirudegarn Saga cards rather than a Saiyan Saga leader.
sources: Wikipedia: Dragon Ball Z: Wrath of the Dragon
CBR: Where Did Trunks Get His Sword
GameRant: Dragon Ball: Where Did Future Trunks Get His Sword
